nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, Tubastraea is found in shaded reef walls, caves, crevices, and on the undersides of overhangs, where direct sunlight rarely reaches. It colonizes artificial structures such as shipwrecks readily, and tends to occur in areas with strong current that delivers planktonic food.

diet.notes: The Tubastraea Sun Coral is entirely heterotrophic, meaning it contains no symbiotic algae and derives no energy from light whatsoever. It must be fed every day, with food delivered directly to each individual polyp. Accepted prey includes frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, copepods, rotifers, and finely chopped clam, scallop, or shrimp. Directing food with a turkey baster or pipette is strongly recommended to ensure each polyp receives an adequate portion. Live copepods are particularly effective at stimulating a feeding response. Amino acid supplements can provide a nutritional boost but do not replace the need for particulate meaty food.

goodWith: other non-photosynthetic corals (Chili Coral, Sea Fans, Tree Coral)
avoidWith: aggressive corals with sweeper tentacles (Galaxea, Hammer, Torch)
fast-growing photosynthetic corals that may overgrow
algae-prone areas where overgrowth can smother the base
food-stealing fish (wrasses, triggers, large angelfish) in feeding proximity

summary: The Tubastraea Sun Coral (Tubastraea sp.) is a non-photosynthetic coral that produces vivid orange or yellow polyps and requires no light for survival. Unlike most reef corals, it gets all of its energy from food rather than sunlight, making daily target-feeding to each individual polyp the single most critical — and demanding — aspect of its care.
careNotes:
Tubastraea is a genus of non-photosynthetic (NPS) large polyp stony corals. Unlike the majority of reef corals, they contain no zooxanthellae — the symbiotic algae that allow most corals to feed through photosynthesis — and must obtain all of their energy from food alone. The most commonly available aquarium species are T. faulkneri, which produces orange or yellow polyps, and T. coccinea, which has smaller orange polyps. T. micranthus forms striking black or brown colonies and is sometimes sold as the Black Sun Coral.

FEEDING — THE PRIMARY CHALLENGE: Daily target-feeding to every individual polyp is the single most critical husbandry requirement, and most aquarium losses are attributed to underfeeding or inconsistent feeding schedules. Polyps naturally extend their tentacles at night, but experienced keepers are generally able to condition them to open at other times by establishing a consistent daily feeding routine. Feeding is done by directing food at each polyp opening using a turkey baster or pipette. Accepted prey includes frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, copepods, and finely chopped seafood. A colony with many polyps may require 10 to 20 minutes of attentive feeding per session. Some advanced hobbyists run automated dosing equipment to deliver a dozen or more small feedings per day for maximum growth, though one to two substantial daily feedings with a baster is a workable approach for most keepers. Allowing more than one to two days between feedings is not recommended.

LIGHTING: The Sun Coral requires no light to sustain itself, and placement in the darkest areas of the tank is acceptable and often preferred. Light levels should be kept below 75 PAR to avoid tissue stress. The colony's vivid color is unrelated to light exposure.

FLOW: Moderate to high water movement benefits the colony by delivering planktonic food to the polyps. Briefly reducing flow in the immediate area during feeding sessions tends to improve polyp capture rates. Positioning the colony a few inches downstream from a powerhead can create a beneficial swirling current that carries food to the tentacles.

PLACEMENT: The Sun Coral can be placed anywhere in the tank, including mounted inverted under overhangs. It should not be placed directly on sand, as contact with shifting substrate can damage tissue. Elevating the colony on stable rockwork is advisable.

NUTRIENT LOAD: Because heavy feeding is required, significant organic waste accumulates over time. An appropriately sized protein skimmer and consistent water changes are generally advised to manage the resulting nitrate and phosphate levels.

FRAGGING: Colonies are relatively straightforward to fragment — sections can be cut with bone cutters, and individual polyps with attached skeleton will typically encrust and regrow on their own.

INVASIVE WARNING: Tubastraea coccinea is established as an invasive species on Caribbean and Atlantic reefs, having spread well beyond its original Indo-Pacific range. It was documented in the Caribbean as early as 1943, likely introduced via ship ballast water, and has since spread north to the Florida Keys. Aquarium specimens, tank water, and any associated materials must never be released into Atlantic, Gulf of Mexico, or Caribbean waters. Proper disposal methods include desiccation, treatment with bleach, or sealed landfill disposal.
breedingNotes: In the wild, Tubastraea reproduces through broadcast spawning, releasing planulae larvae that settle and form new colonies. In aquaria, sexual reproduction is not reliably achieved. Fragmentation is the most accessible propagation method available to hobbyists: colonies can be divided with bone cutters, and individual polyp clusters with attached skeleton will typically encrust a new surface and grow. The genus's capacity for rapid larval settlement and asexual spread is widely considered one of the factors behind its success as an invasive species on Atlantic and Caribbean reefs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Orange Tube Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12), lighting level (low), flow (medium to strong), placement (bottom), care level (moderate), feeding method (target-feed each polyp). Temperature converted to Celsius: 72°F = 22.2°C, 78°F = 25.6°C.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Sun Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 77–80°F / 25–26.7°C, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, calcium ~425 ppm, magnesium 1200–1350 ppm), lighting max 75 PAR, flow moderate 20–40x turnover, minimum tank 10 gal (38 L), colony size ~6 in diameter (15 cm), feeding 3–5x/week minimum. Temperature upper end 26.7°C averaged with LiveAquaria lower end to arrive at 24–26°C range. Care level listed as 'moderate'; bumped to 'advanced' per feeding-burden and invasive-awareness factors.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens — Tubastraea Coral Care
additional.notes: Expert-level specialist source. Confirmed PAR under 75, calcium ~400 ppm, magnesium ~1350 ppm, kH 8–11 (wild ocean ~8–9). Detailed feeding guidance: up to dozens of small feedings per day for optimal growth; colony can be conditioned to open during daytime with consistent feeding. Care level described as 'very high maintenance — not recommended unless reef keeper is really committed.' Confirmed Indo-Pacific native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Tubastraea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Dendrophylliidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range context, invasive status of T. coccinea (Caribbean since 1943, Florida Keys by 2004), and species identification (T. coccinea, T. faulkneri, T. micranthus). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — Keeping and Feeding the Sun Corals
additional.notes: Confirmed NPS classification, placement under overhangs/cave ceilings, strong flow for food delivery, importance of live copepods (tigger pods) as prey. Emphasized 'The key to success is proper nutrition.' Used as supporting source for care notes prose.
